title: Hydraulic System Energy Loss Calculator
description: Industrial-grade tool to calculate total energy loss in hydraulic systems, identify loss drivers, and suggest improvement actions based on ISO 4409, Lean, and Six Sigma principles.

inputs.label: Flow Rate
inputs.businessContext: Volumetric flow rate of hydraulic fluid through the system. Typical range for industrial systems: 10-500 L/min.

businessContext_i18n.en: Volumetric flow rate of hydraulic fluid through the system. Typical range for industrial systems: 10-500 L/min.

inputs.label: Total Pressure Drop
inputs.businessContext: Sum of all pressure losses across components (pipes, valves, fittings). ISO 4409 recommends measurement at steady state.

businessContext_i18n.en: Sum of all pressure losses across components (pipes, valves, fittings). ISO 4409 recommends measurement at steady state.

inputs.label: Fluid Density
inputs.businessContext: Density of hydraulic fluid at operating temperature. Typical mineral oil: 850-900 kg/m³.

businessContext_i18n.en: Density of hydraulic fluid at operating temperature. Typical mineral oil: 850-900 kg/m³.

inputs.label: Kinematic Viscosity
inputs.businessContext: Fluid viscosity at operating temperature. ISO VG 46 is common for industrial hydraulics.

businessContext_i18n.en: Fluid viscosity at operating temperature. ISO VG 46 is common for industrial hydraulics.

inputs.label: Total Pipe Length
inputs.businessContext: Total length of hydraulic piping in the system. Longer runs increase friction losses.

businessContext_i18n.en: Total length of hydraulic piping in the system. Longer runs increase friction losses.

inputs.label: Pipe Inner Diameter
inputs.businessContext: Inner diameter of the main hydraulic pipe. Affects flow velocity and Reynolds number.

businessContext_i18n.en: Inner diameter of the main hydraulic pipe. Affects flow velocity and Reynolds number.

inputs.label: Number of Valves
inputs.businessContext: Total number of directional, pressure, and flow control valves in the system.

businessContext_i18n.en: Total number of directional, pressure, and flow control valves in the system.

inputs.label: Number of Fittings
inputs.businessContext: Total number of elbows, tees, reducers, and other fittings.

businessContext_i18n.en: Total number of elbows, tees, reducers, and other fittings.

inputs.label: System Type
inputs.businessContext: Application type influences typical loss factors and maintenance intervals.

businessContext_i18n.en: Application type influences typical loss factors and maintenance intervals.

inputs.label: New System (less than 1 year in service)
inputs.businessContext: New systems may have different loss characteristics due to break-in period.

label_i18n.en: New System (less than 1 year in service)

businessContext_i18n.en: New systems may have different loss characteristics due to break-in period.

rules.message: Flow rate and pressure drop must both be positive for energy loss calculation.
Kinematic viscosity and pipe diameter must be positive to compute Reynolds number.
Pipe length must be non-negative and diameter positive.

thresholds.message: Total energy loss exceeds 5000 W. Immediate system audit recommended.
Flow is laminar (Re < 2000). Consider increasing flow or reducing viscosity to improve efficiency.
Darcy friction factor exceeds 0.04. Check for pipe roughness or undersized diameter.
Valve losses account for more than 30% of total loss. Review valve sizing and type.

formulas.label: Flow Velocity
formulas.description: Mean fluid velocity in the pipe.

formulas.label: Darcy Friction Factor
formulas.description: Friction factor using Swamee-Jain approximation for turbulent flow (Re > 4000), else Poiseuille for laminar.

formulas.label: Pipe Friction Loss
formulas.description: Pressure loss due to pipe friction (Darcy-Weisbach).

formulas.label: Minor Losses (Valves & Fittings)
formulas.description: Pressure loss through valves and fittings using equivalent length method.

formulas.label: Total Pressure Loss
formulas.description: Sum of pipe friction and minor losses.

primary.label: Total Energy Loss
primary.description: Total hydraulic power dissipated as heat due to friction and minor losses.

pipe_friction_loss_watts.label: Pipe Friction Loss
pipe_friction_loss_watts.description: Energy loss due to pipe wall friction.

minor_losses_watts.label: Minor Losses (Valves & Fittings)
minor_losses_watts.description: Energy loss due to valves and fittings.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: High Friction Factor
hiddenLossDrivers.description: Friction factor above 0.04 indicates potential pipe roughness or undersized diameter.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: Laminar Flow Regime
hiddenLossDrivers.description: Reynolds number below 2000 indicates laminar flow, which may be less efficient for heat transfer but can increase viscous losses.

hiddenLossDrivers.label: Excessive Valve Loss
hiddenLossDrivers.description: Valve losses contribute more than 30% of total loss.

suggestedActions.label: Increase Pipe Diameter
suggestedActions.description: Larger diameter reduces flow velocity and friction losses. Consider upsizing if velocity > 5 m/s.

suggestedActions.label: Reduce Valve Count or Use Low-Loss Valves
suggestedActions.description: Minimize number of valves or replace with low-pressure-drop designs.

suggestedActions.label: Optimize Fluid Viscosity
suggestedActions.description: Use lower viscosity fluid if operating temperature allows, to reduce friction losses.

suggestedActions.label: Clean or Replace Pipes
suggestedActions.description: High friction factor may indicate internal roughness or deposits. Inspect and clean.

dataConfidenceAdjusted.label: Data Confidence Adjusted Energy Loss
dataConfidenceAdjusted.description: Energy loss adjusted for input data confidence (e.g., using typical uncertainty of ±5% for flow and pressure).
